justification: All pass criteria met: 5 services recommended with strong justification; Location Tracking isolated for 5k writes/sec with CQRS and time-series store; Payment isolated for strict consistency and PCI boundary; team size constraint explicitly addressed ('8 engineers cannot sustain more than 5 services'); Strangler Fig migration pattern recommended with phased timeline; trade_offs covers operational overhead, distributed tracing complexity, and eventual consistency debugging; risks includes distributed transaction failures (Order+Payment), operational maturity gap, and event bus SPOF.

observations.text: Excellent team-size reasoning: explicitly calculates 1.6 engineers per service and explains why 6 would leave single-owner services with no backup
Customer & Notification merge is well-justified (shared data, same change cadence) with a clear split trigger (team grows to 12+)
Strangler Fig ordering is thoughtful — Location first due to lowest domain coupling and highest independent scaling need
Distributed transaction risk explicitly addressed with PENDING_PAYMENT state machine and idempotency keys
Pitfalls section warns against shared common libraries, simultaneous extraction, shared databases, and distributed transactions — all real-world failure modes
Context map with DDD relationship types (Customer-Supplier, Conformist, ACL) adds architectural rigor beyond the minimum
Flexibility vs rigidity points clearly distinguish what should evolve from what must remain stable
Could have more explicitly called out PCI compliance as a Payment isolation driver — mentioned 'strict consistency' but PCI DSS scope reduction is only implied, not named

agent_output_excerpt:
## Executive Summary

Decompose the monolith into **5 services** aligned to domain boundaries, not 6 (resist the urge to split every capability into its own service — 8 engineers cannot sustain more than 5 services operationally). The critical architectural decisions are: (1) isolate Location Tracking as an independent, high-throughput service with its own data store optimized for time-series writes, (2) keep Payment Processing as a separate service with its own transactional database to enforce
